Title: Plan Your Week Backwards From Results (Outcome-First OS)
Teaser: Design your whole week backwards from 3 concrete Friday outcomes, with energy-matched time blocks and a ruthless drop list.
PromptHint: List your 3 real outcomes in [OUTCOMES]. Add commitments in [FIXED EVENTS] and your peak hours in [ENERGY MAP].
Prompt:
You are my elite chief-of-staff and a planning systems designer. Build my week BACKWARDS from results, not a to-do list.

DESIRED OUTCOMES BY FRIDAY (what 'done' looks like): [OUTCOMES]
FIXED EVENTS / MEETINGS I can't move: [FIXED EVENTS]
MY ENERGY MAP (when I focus best vs crash): [ENERGY MAP]

THINK in this order, silently, then output:
1) PUSH BACK: For each outcome, if it's vague (e.g. 'make progress on X'), rewrite it as a verifiable deliverable with a definition of done. Flag every vague one.
2) REVERSE-ENGINEER: Decompose each outcome into the minimum sequence of tasks that produces it.
3) ENERGY-MATCH: Tag every task High-focus / Medium-focus / Recovery-admin and place it where my energy fits.
4) PROTECT: Reserve deep-work blocks first; fit admin into the cracks.
5) DROP LIST: Name what I should NOT do this week to protect the outcomes.

OUTPUT FORMAT (use these labelled sections):
## Sharpened Outcomes (with definition of done)
## Mon-Fri Time-Blocked Schedule (markdown table: Day | Time | Block | Task | Focus level | Linked outcome)
## The ONE keystone task (do this first or the week unravels)
## Drop / Defer / Delegate list
## Friday self-check questions (5 yes/no)
Be blunt. If my outcomes don't fit the time I have, tell me and propose a cut.

Title: The Brutal Productivity Mirror: Find What's Quietly Holding You Back
Teaser: Turn the model's memory of you into a sharp, specific read of the patterns that quietly sabotage you, then hand you the one fix that matters.
PromptHint: Paste recurring frustrations, goals and work habits in [ABOUT ME] (or run on memory). Set candor with [INTENSITY].
Prompt:
Act as a no-nonsense executive coach who has watched me work for a year and is done being polite. Your job is insight, not validation.

WHAT YOU KNOW ABOUT ME (habits, goals, personality, recurring frustrations): [ABOUT ME]
CANDOR LEVEL (gentle / direct / brutal): [INTENSITY]

RULES:
- Be specific to ME, not generic productivity advice. Quote patterns, not platitudes.
- Name the costly pattern, then the hidden payoff I get from keeping it (why I haven't changed).
- No flattery sandwich. No 'but you're doing great.' Earn trust by being right, not nice.
- This is self-reflection coaching, not a diagnosis; you are not a therapist or doctor.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
## Top 5 Blind Spots (each: the pattern -> the evidence -> what it's costing me -> the hidden payoff)
## The single self-sabotage loop I repeat most
## One uncomfortable truth I'm avoiding
## The 1 keystone change that would unlock the most (and the first 10-minute action today)
End with one sentence that would make me put the phone down and actually start.

Title: Make Any Hard Decision: The AI Interviews You First
Teaser: For any hard call, the AI interviews you one question at a time, guesses your answer so you can just confirm, then builds a weighted framework and a verdict.
PromptHint: State the decision in [DECISION]. The AI runs the interview itself; just answer each question.
Prompt:
You are a decision strategist. Do NOT give advice yet. First, interview me to surface what actually matters.

THE DECISION I'm facing: [DECISION]

INTERVIEW PROTOCOL:
- Ask me ONE question at a time, and after each question propose the answer you'd guess for me so I can just say 'yes, go with that' or correct you.
- Cover: the real goal underneath this decision, hard constraints, what I'm optimizing for, my risk tolerance, the options on the table, reversibility, my time horizon, and what would make me regret each path.
- Keep going until you have enough to decide well (typically 6-9 questions). Tell me when you have enough.

WHEN DONE, output (and label clearly):
## Decision criteria (weighted 1-5, with why each weight)
## Options scored against criteria (markdown table: Option | each criterion | weighted total)
## The strongest case for the runner-up (steelman it)
## My recommended call + confidence % + the one thing that would flip it
## A reversible first step I can take this week to test it
This is a thinking aid, not professional financial, legal, or medical advice. Begin with question 1 now.

Title: Stress-Test Your Plan Like a Skeptic Who Wants It to Fail
Teaser: Hand over any plan or goal and get it attacked by a skeptic who wants it to fail, plus the exact conditions under which you'd be wrong.
PromptHint: Paste your plan in [PLAN]. Optionally name the timeframe/stakes in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
Challenge my thinking. You are a sharp, skeptical advisor whose job is to find why this fails, not to encourage me. Do not soften your critique and do not end on a reassuring note.

MY PLAN / GOAL: [PLAN]
CONTEXT (timeframe, stakes, resources): [CONTEXT]

DO THIS:
1) Restate my plan in one sentence so I know you understood it (if it's vague, say so).
2) Attack it like someone who has seen this exact plan fail before.
3) Separate fatal flaws from fixable ones.
4) Tell me what I'd have to believe for this to work — and how likely each belief is.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
## The 3 strongest counterarguments (ranked by how badly they hurt)
## Hidden assumptions I'm treating as facts
## The conditions under which I am clearly WRONG
## Failure modes (most likely way this dies) + an early warning signal for each
## What I'd do if I were betting against me
## The minimum changes that would make me change my mind from 'pass' to 'fund it'
No hedging, no 'it depends' without saying what it depends on. Be right, not kind.

Title: Life Audit: Where Your Time Betrays Your Priorities
Teaser: Map where your hours actually go against what you say matters most, then get the single biggest gap surfaced with a 30-day correction.
PromptHint: List your stated priorities in [PRIORITIES] and a typical week's time use in [TIME LOG]. Name the life areas in [AREAS].
Prompt:
You are a life-design analyst running a rigorous time audit. Find the gap between what I SAY matters and where my hours actually go.

MY STATED PRIORITIES (in rank order): [PRIORITIES]
HOW A TYPICAL WEEK ACTUALLY GOES (rough hours by activity): [TIME LOG]
LIFE AREAS to consider: [AREAS]

METHOD:
1) Bucket my time into the life areas. Estimate hours/% for each.
2) Compare each area's time share to its priority rank.
3) Compute a 'mismatch score' per area = stated importance minus actual time invested.
4) Flag the single biggest misalignment and the sneakiest time-sink that pretends to be productive.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
## Time-vs-Priority table (markdown: Area | Stated rank | Actual % of week | Mismatch | Verdict)
## The #1 mismatch — what it's quietly costing me
## 3 'fake-productive' activities to cut or cap
## A reallocation plan: where to move 5 hours/week and from where
## A 30-day correction with one weekly checkpoint
Be honest even if the biggest mismatch is uncomfortable. This is reflection, not professional financial or medical advice.

Title: Your AI Chief of Staff: A Daily Brief That Names the ONE Thing
Teaser: Drop in your calendar, threads and projects and get a blunt morning brief that names the single highest-leverage move, drafts your two hardest replies, and tells you what to drop.
PromptHint: Paste today's calendar in [CALENDAR], top projects in [PROJECTS], and open threads/messages in [INBOX].
Prompt:
Act as my chief of staff for today. You are blunt, protective of my focus, and allergic to busywork. Triage everything and force prioritization.

TODAY'S CALENDAR: [CALENDAR]
TOP PROJECTS / GOALS: [PROJECTS]
OPEN THREADS, MESSAGES, DEADLINES: [INBOX]

DO THIS:
1) Identify the ONE thing that, if done today, makes the rest of the week easier or unnecessary.
2) Flag the 3 real fires worth my attention (and explicitly which 'urgent' items are not).
3) Draft replies to the 2 most time-sensitive messages (concise, in my voice — short sentences, no corporate fluff).
4) Build a focus-protecting, time-blocked plan that fits my actual calendar gaps.
5) Be honest about what I should drop, defer, or delegate today.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
## The ONE thing (and why it's the leverage point)
## Today's 3 fires (+ what's pretending to be a fire)
## Time-blocked plan (table: Time | Block | Task | Why now)
## 2 drafted replies (ready to send)
## Drop / Defer / Delegate
## End-of-day done-test: 3 checkboxes that mean today was a win

Title: Friday Weekly Review That Turns Last Week's Mess Into a Plan
Teaser: A repeatable GTD-style weekly review that turns last week's chaos into banked wins, honest slippage, and a named #1 outcome for next week.
PromptHint: Paste last week's notes/results in [LAST WEEK], next week's calendar in [NEXT WEEK], and goals in [BIG GOALS].
Prompt:
Run my weekly review like a disciplined operator. The goal is a clean reset and a focused plan — not a feel-good recap.

WHAT HAPPENED LAST WEEK (wins, misses, loose ends): [LAST WEEK]
NEXT WEEK'S CALENDAR & COMMITMENTS: [NEXT WEEK]
MY BIGGER GOALS this quarter: [BIG GOALS]

PROCESS (capture -> clarify -> organize -> reflect -> plan):
1) Pull every open loop into one list; mark each as Done, Drop, Delegate, or Carry.
2) Compare last week's intentions to reality and name the slippage honestly (no excuses).
3) Surface 1-2 patterns repeating week over week.
4) Tie next week to my quarter goals and name the single most important outcome.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
## Wins worth banking (and why they mattered)
## Slippage — what slipped and the real reason (be candid)
## Recurring pattern to fix
## Open loops (table: Item | Decision | Next action | When)
## Next week's #1 outcome + 3 supporting priorities
## One thing to stop doing
Close with a single sentence I'd pin above my desk for the week.

Title: Actually Understand Anything: The Feynman 4-Layer Teach-Back
Teaser: Learn any topic through four escalating explanations, then teach it back so the AI hunts your gaps and re-teaches only the weak spots.
PromptHint: Name what you want to learn in [TOPIC]. Add your current level in [LEVEL] and why you need it in [GOAL].
Prompt:
Act as a breakthrough learning architect using the Feynman technique. Make me genuinely understand, not just nod along.

TOPIC I want to master: [TOPIC]
MY CURRENT LEVEL: [LEVEL]
WHY I NEED IT / how I'll use it: [GOAL]

TEACH IN 4 LAYERS, each building on the last:
1) To a curious 12-year-old, using ONE vivid analogy.
2) To a smart beginner, adding the real terms and why they exist.
3) To a capable peer in the field, with the mechanism and common misconceptions.
4) At expert depth, with edge cases, trade-offs, and where the simple model breaks.

THEN run the teach-back loop:
- Ask me to explain the concept back to you in my own words.
- Find EXACTLY where my explanation breaks or goes fuzzy.
- Re-teach ONLY those gap points (don't repeat what I got right).
- Repeat until I can explain it cleanly, then confirm I'm done.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
## Layer 1-4 explanations (clearly labelled)
## The 3 ideas most people get wrong here
## Your turn: teach it back to me (prompt me)
## [After my attempt] Gap report + targeted re-teach
## 5 active-recall questions to test retention tomorrow
Keep analogies concrete and avoid jargon until Layer 2.

Title: Build a Habit That Survives Your Worst Day (Not Just Your Best)
Teaser: Engineer a habit that actually sticks using cue-routine-reward, identity framing, friction tuning, and a relapse-proof 'never miss twice' rule.
PromptHint: Name the habit you want in [HABIT]. Add your daily routine in [ROUTINE] and past failures in [WHAT FAILED].
Prompt:
You are a behavior-design coach (BJ Fogg + Atomic Habits school). Design a habit engineered to survive bad days, not just motivated ones.

HABIT I want to build (or break): [HABIT]
MY DAILY ROUTINE & anchors I already do reliably: [ROUTINE]
WHAT'S FAILED BEFORE and why I think it failed: [WHAT FAILED]

DESIGN PRINCIPLES to apply:
- Make it tiny enough to do on my worst day (the 'too small to fail' version).
- Anchor it to an existing trigger I never skip.
- Reduce friction for the good habit; add friction for the bad one.
- Frame it as an identity ('I'm the kind of person who...'), not a chore.
- Plan for the inevitable miss with a 'never miss twice' recovery rule.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
## Identity statement (one line)
## The tiny version + the full version (and when to scale up)
## Cue -> Routine -> Reward loop (table)
## Friction changes (add / remove, environment tweaks)
## Implementation intention (one line, 'When (situation), I will (tiny action)')
## Relapse plan: early warning sign + the 2-minute recovery action
## A simple 30-day tracking method I can actually maintain
Diagnose specifically why my past attempts died and design around that failure mode.

Title: Schedule Your Day by Energy, Not the Clock
Teaser: The AI asks when your brain peaks and crashes, then sorts every task into High-focus, Medium-focus, and Recovery blocks that fit reality.
PromptHint: Dump your task list in [TASKS]. Add fixed commitments in [FIXED] and your known energy pattern in [ENERGY] if you have it.
Prompt:
You are a productivity scientist who schedules by ENERGY, not just by clock. Match the right task to the right brain state.

MY TASK LIST for the day/week: [TASKS]
FIXED COMMITMENTS I can't move: [FIXED]
WHAT I KNOW ABOUT MY ENERGY (optional): [ENERGY]

STEP 1 — If my energy pattern is unclear, ask me 3 quick questions: when I think most clearly, when I reliably crash, and whether I'm a morning or night brain. Wait for answers.
STEP 2 — Classify each task by cognitive load: High-focus (deep, creative, hard decisions), Medium-focus (meetings, structured work), Recovery/admin (email, errands, low-stakes).
STEP 3 — Place High-focus tasks in my peak windows, Recovery tasks in my troughs, and protect at least one uninterrupted deep block.
STEP 4 — Add buffers and a hard stop so the day doesn't bleed.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
## Your energy curve (as I understand it)
## Task classification (table: Task | Load | Best window)
## Time-blocked schedule (table: Time | Energy state | Block | Task)
## The one task to do at peak energy no matter what
## Trough-time 'good enough' tasks I can do tired
## A 1-line shutdown ritual to end the day
If I have more high-focus tasks than peak hours, tell me what to cut or move to tomorrow.

Title: Turn One Big Goal Into a 90-Day Roadmap (Backwards-Planned)
Teaser: Turn a fuzzy 90-day ambition into milestones, weekly targets, leading indicators, and a pre-mortem of what will most likely derail it.
PromptHint: State the 90-day goal in [GOAL]. Add your current starting point in [START] and weekly time available in [TIME].
Prompt:
You are a strategy coach who turns ambitions into shippable quarters. Build a 90-day plan by working BACKWARDS from the finish line.

MY 90-DAY GOAL: [GOAL]
WHERE I'M STARTING from today: [START]
TIME I realistically have per week: [TIME]

METHOD:
1) Sharpen the goal into a measurable, dated finish line. If it's vague, make it concrete and tell me what you changed.
2) Reverse-plan: define the Day-90 state, then the 3 monthly milestones that must be true to reach it, then the weekly targets.
3) Identify LEADING indicators (weekly actions I control) vs lagging outcomes (results I don't fully control).
4) Run a pre-mortem: assume it's Day 90 and I failed — list the most likely reasons and a guardrail for each.
5) Pressure-test against my weekly time budget and flag if the plan is unrealistic.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
## Sharpened goal (measurable + dated)
## Milestone map (table: Month | Milestone | Done = ...)
## Weekly cadence (what I do every week — leading actions)
## Leading vs lagging metrics to track
## Pre-mortem: top 4 failure reasons + a guardrail each
## Week-1 starting block (3 concrete actions)
Be honest if the goal won't fit the time; propose a smaller version that still matters.

Title: Reclaim Your Focus: A Personal Deep-Work & Distraction Defense Plan
Teaser: Diagnose exactly what's shredding your attention, then get a custom deep-work protocol, an environment redesign, and a single-task focus ritual.
PromptHint: Describe what you're trying to focus on in [DEEP WORK]. List your biggest distractions in [DISTRACTIONS] and your context in [SETUP].
Prompt:
You are a focus and attention coach trained on Cal Newport's Deep Work and modern attention science. Engineer my environment and routine so concentrated work becomes the path of least resistance — not a daily fight against willpower.

WHAT I'M TRYING TO DO DEEP WORK ON: [DEEP WORK]
MY BIGGEST DISTRACTIONS (apps, people, internal urges, notifications): [DISTRACTIONS]
MY WORK CONTEXT (where I work, hours available, devices, constraints): [SETUP]

THINK in this order, silently, then output:
1) DIAGNOSE: For each distraction, classify it as external (interruptions, pings) or internal (boredom, anxiety, novelty-seeking), and name the underlying need it's meeting.
2) PROTOCOL: Design a deep-work block protocol I can run daily — length, ramp-up ritual, single-tasking rule, and how I handle the urge to switch.
3) ENVIRONMENT: Redesign my space and devices to remove friction from focus and add friction to every distraction (specific app, phone, and tab changes).
4) RECOVERY: Give me a 30-second reset for when I notice I've drifted, instead of spiraling into guilt.
5) MEASURE: Define what a 'won' focus session looks like so I can track it.

OUTPUT FORMAT (use these labelled sections):
## Distraction diagnosis (table: Distraction | Type | Underlying need | The cheap fix)
## My daily deep-work protocol (start ritual -> focus block -> break -> shutdown)
## Environment & device redesign (concrete add-friction / remove-friction changes)
## The 30-second drift-recovery move
## My single focus ritual to start every session the same way
## A 1-week focus experiment with one metric to track
Be specific to MY distractions and setup — no generic 'just turn off notifications' advice. If my goal needs more uninterrupted time than my setup allows, say so and tell me what to renegotiate.

Title: Build Your Second Brain: A PARA Knowledge System That Compounds
Teaser: Stand up a personal knowledge system that ingests your notes and sources, files them by PARA, links them, and surfaces fresh connections instead of letting them rot.
PromptHint: Tell it the areas you care about in [AREAS] and paste your first batch of notes/links in [SOURCES].
Prompt:
You are my second-brain librarian. Help me build a persistent, interlinked knowledge system using Tiago Forte's PARA method (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archives) so my notes compound instead of rotting.

AREAS OF MY LIFE/WORK I want to organize: [AREAS]
FIRST BATCH OF SOURCES (notes, links, dumps) to ingest: [SOURCES]
CURRENT MESS (where my notes live now, optional): [CURRENT SETUP]

STEP 1 — Propose an initial PARA structure tailored to my areas: active Projects (with an end date/outcome), ongoing Areas (standards to maintain), Resources (topics of interest), Archives (inactive).
STEP 2 — For each source I paste, extract atomic claims/ideas, tag them by topic, and file them into the structure.
STEP 3 — Maintain an index/table of contents, surface NEW connections between this batch and what we already have, and flag contradictions.
STEP 4 — Tell me what's missing and ask what to ingest next.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown I can paste into any notes app):
## Proposed PARA structure (folder tree)
## Ingested notes (per source: atomic points + tags + where it's filed)
## Updated index / table of contents
## New connections & contradictions spotted
## Suggested next captures (3)
Write notes as clean markdown with descriptive titles and [[wiki-style links]] between related notes. Start by confirming the structure before filing.
